title: Testing the General Deductive Reasoning Capacity of Large Language Models Using OOD Examples
authors: Abulhair Saparov, Richard Yuanzhe Pang, Vishakh Padmakumar, Nitish Joshi, Seyed Mehran Kazemi, Najoung Kim, He He
venue: Published as a conference paper at NeurIPS 2023 (per arXiv 'Comments' field); arXiv 2305.15269, v1 24 May 2023 / v2 20 Oct 2023 / v3 3 Nov 2023. Affiliations: NYU (Saparov, Pang, Padmakumar, Joshi), Google (Kazemi, Kim), Boston University (Kim).
claim: Given the intractably large space of proofs, a model with general deductive reasoning ability must generalize to proofs more complex than those it was shown; the paper introduces PrOntoQA-OOD, a programmable synthetic dataset extending PrOntoQA with a complete set of natural-deduction rules (not just modus ponens) and controllable depth, width, and compositionality, to test this directly. Across four LLMs (GPT-3.5, PaLM 540B, LLaMA 65B, FLAN-T5 11B) under 8-shot CoT prompting, the paper finds LLMs generalize well to compositional proofs (combining multiple rule types) but degrade with increasing proof depth, and require explicit in-context demonstrations to produce hypothetical subproofs (proof by cases / proof by contradiction) rather than acquiring these from pretraining alone.
novelty: Authors present PrOntoQA-OOD as extending PrOntoQA (Saparov and He, 2023) in three ways versus prior deduction-reasoning benchmarks (CLUTRR, LogiQA, ProofWriter, FOLIO -- compared explicitly in Table 1): (1) a complete set of natural-deduction rules over all propositional connectives (conjunction, disjunction, implication, negation via proof by contradiction), not just modus ponens; (2) fully programmable/controllable proof depth and width as generation parameters, enabling systematic depth- and width-generalization tests; (3) a recursive procedure to generate compositional proofs combining multiple distinct deduction rules in one proof, enabling compositional-generalization tests. The paper also contributes a formal, automated CoT-correctness evaluator (semantic parse each step to FOL, check each step follows validly and immediately from its premises, check a valid path exists from premises to goal) rather than relying on label-only accuracy, which the authors argue prior datasets largely lack (Section 2, Table 1). A further claimed contribution is the empirical finding that ICL generalization behaves differently from supervised learning (best in-context demos are sometimes NOT drawn from the test distribution), which the authors say is not explained by existing Bayesian-inference or gradient-descent theories of ICL (Section 5).

protocol.description: 8-shot chain-of-thought prompting (Section 4): the model is given 8 in-context CoT demonstrations (premises + query + full gold proof as CoT) followed by one test question (premises + query), and is asked to produce a full CoT proof in a single forward generation -- there is no multi-turn interaction loop or per-step feedback to the model. Two demonstration-distribution settings are compared: in-demonstration (ID, demos drawn from same distribution as the test example) and out-of-demonstration (OOD, demos drawn from a different distribution, e.g. missing the test example's deduction rule). Footnote 5 states 8-shot was chosen 'following PrOntoQA, since adding further in-context examples did not improve performance' -- for the depth/width generalization experiments (Figure 7) only 4 in-context examples are used ('In these experiments, there are 4 rather than 8 in-context examples,' Figure 7 caption).
protocol.observation_content: unverified — missing from extraction: the exact prompt template/text is not reproduced in the local extraction. The worked example (Figure 10, 'An example from PrOntoQA-OOD... The question is shown in blue, the query in red, and the chain-of-thought/answer in green') and the CoT-to-logic evaluation worked example in Section 3 are both rendered as unreproduced images -- extraction_notes states figures/algorithm diagrams are not reproduced, only captions, and the Section 3 evaluation figure caption itself is 'none found' in the source HTML. What can be verified textually: each example contains 'a handful of premises, a query (the target fact to be proved/disproved), and a gold CoT containing the proof of the query' (Section 3, 'A programmable dataset'), fictional names are used for all concepts ('wumpus' instead of 'cat', Section 3), and distractor sentences are added so the model 'is forced to choose the correct premise from two options for the next deduction step' (Section 3, 'Adding distractors'). Full literal prompt text (system/user framing, exact delimiters) is not present in the extraction.

representations: The paper does not compare alternative input/serialization formats (e.g. natural language vs symbolic/FOL) for the same content as an experimental variable -- proofs are always presented as natural-language sentences (Section 3, Table 2 'Natural language example' column) with an underlying formal tree representation used only for generation and grading, not as an alternative prompt format given to the model (Figure 2 caption shows the same proof 'in both natural language and a formal tree representation' for illustration, not as a compared condition). The one representational axis that IS varied and measured is sentence ORDER and distractor presence/absence in the in-context examples vs test example (Section 4.3, Figure 8/9/13): (a) both ID and test examples have distractors with all sentences randomly ordered; (b) distractors removed from in-context examples only, with in-context sentence order fixed to a postorder traversal of the ontology tree while test sentences remain randomly ordered; (c) same as (b) but distractors in in-context examples replaced with irrelevant sentences instead of removed. Effect: 'The models' performance is largely unaffected, with the exception of a few rules for specific models' (Section 4.3) -- i.e., removing distractors from demonstrations while keeping them in the test example did NOT broadly hurt OOD generalization, contrary to a supervised-learning-style overfitting prediction; the one documented failure is GPT-3.5 on implication/disjunction elimination, which 'copies many of the distractor sentences into the output, likely due to the fact that it has learned to apply a copying heuristic from the in-context demonstrations' (Section 4.3, Figure 9).

models.name: GPT-3.5 (text-davinci-003), ~175B
strategies: in-demonstration (ID) vs out-of-demonstration (OOD) in-context example distributions
models.key_results: Performs best overall among the 4 models on the per-rule ID test (Section 4.1, Figure 4 top). In the OOD unseen-rule test (Section 4.2.1, Figure 4 bottom), GPT-3.5 is the only model able to use proof by contradiction without in-context demonstrations, by substituting modus tollens reasoning (Section 4.2.1) -- contrasted with McKenzie et al. (2022)'s finding of inverse scaling for modus tollens. GPT-3.5 performs worse on OOD disjunction elimination, 'possibly due to the fact that there is no equivalent alternate rule' (Section 4.2.1, error example in Figure 5/14). On compositional generalization (Section 4.2.2, Figure 6), GPT-3.5 (and PaLM) 'have difficulty when the number of rule types is 4' (example error in Figure 6/15). On depth/width generalization (Section 4.2.3, Figure 7), GPT-3.5 is the 'notable exception' where ID accuracy on conjunction elimination remains high even as OOD (depth-generalization) accuracy decreases, and GPT-3.5 is the only model able to generalize to greater proof widths on conjunction introduction. On distractors (Section 4.3, Figure 8/9), GPT-3.5 is the only model shown to acquire a distractor-copying heuristic (for implication and disjunction elimination) when in-context demos lack distractors but the test example has them.

models.name: PaLM, 540B
models.key_results: Model size does not strongly correlate with performance; PaLM does not clearly outperform smaller LLaMA (Section 4.1). Sometimes performs BETTER in the OOD compositional setting than ID (Section 4.2.2): 'PaLM, LLaMA, and FLAN-T5 sometimes perform better in the OOD setting than in the ID setting, showing that, in ICL, it is not always best to provide demonstrations from the same distribution as the test example.' Has difficulty at 4 rule types in compositional proofs, same as GPT-3.5 (Section 4.2.2).

models.name: LLaMA, 65B
models.key_results: 'LLaMA performs comparably to PaLM, despite being smaller' (Section 4.1) -- cited as the paper's headline evidence that model size does not strongly correlate with performance. Also shows the sometimes-better-OOD-than-ID compositional pattern (Section 4.2.2).

models.name: FLAN-T5, 11B
models.key_results: 'FLAN-T5 is smaller and performs reasonably on implication and conjunction elimination, but is not able to learn the other deduction rules' (Section 4.1). Zero-Delta-accuracy for FLAN-T5 on some rules in the OOD unseen-rule test is attributable to zero absolute accuracy on those rules to begin with (Section 4.1 caveat on Figure 4). Also exhibits the sometimes-better-OOD-than-ID compositional pattern (Section 4.2.2).

headline_numbers: unverified — missing from extraction for exact percentage values: all quantitative results in this paper (per-rule accuracy, OOD delta-accuracy, compositional accuracy, depth/width accuracy curves, distractor-condition accuracy) are reported ONLY as bar/line charts in Figures 4, 6, 7, 8, 11, 12, 13 -- the local extraction captures figure captions verbatim but the figures themselves (images with the actual accuracy values/bars) are not reproduced as text or numeric tables. No exact percentage-point numbers appear anywhere in the extracted running text. Qualitative headline findings that ARE stated in prose, each with its own section ref: (1) 'CoT is able to elicit out-of-demonstration (OOD) reasoning in LLMs generalizing to compositional proofs' (Section 1, point 1; detailed in Section 4.2.2/Figure 6: 'in all but three experiments, we observe that the gap in proof accuracy between the ID and OOD settings is close to zero'); (2) 'the LLMs do require in-demonstration examples for some deduction rules, specifically, proof by cases and proof by contradiction' (Section 1, point 3; Section 4.2.1: 'no model is able to use disjunction elimination and proof by contradiction without demonstrations'); (3) 'Model size does not strongly correlate with performance' (Section 1, point 4; Section 4.1); (4) depth generalization degrades: 'when shown demonstrations of proofs of depth 2, the models' performance decreases with increasing depth. But this is due to the increase in the inherent difficulty of the task, as both ID and OOD accuracies decrease with increasing depth' (Section 4.2.3, Figure 7); (5) distractor removal from demonstrations largely does not hurt OOD generalization: 'The models' performance is largely unaffected, with the exception of a few rules for specific models' (Section 4.3, Figure 8).
failure_modes: No model (including GPT-3.5) can perform disjunction elimination (proof by cases) or proof by contradiction correctly when these rules are absent from the in-context demonstrations -- 'no model is able to use disjunction elimination and proof by contradiction without demonstrations' (Section 4.2.1).

GPT-3.5 error example (Figure 5/14): on an OOD disjunction-elimination test, GPT-3.5 produces an invalid proof (specific invalid steps shown in red in the figure; full example in Appendix Figure 14). The paper attributes GPT-3.5's weaker OOD disjunction-elimination performance to the absence of an 'equivalent alternate rule' it could substitute (unlike proof by contradiction, where it substitutes modus tollens) (Section 4.2.1).

GPT-3.5 and PaLM both fail on compositional proofs once the number of distinct rule types reaches 4 (Section 4.2.2); a specific incorrect GPT-3.5 output on a min-depth-2, 4-rule-type OOD example is shown in Figure 6 (full example Figure 15).

Distractor-copying heuristic: when in-context demonstrations lack distractors but the test example has them, GPT-3.5 'copies many of the distractor sentences into the output, likely due to the fact that it has learned to apply a copying heuristic from the in-context demonstrations' -- shown for implication and disjunction elimination (Section 4.3, Figure 9, full example Figure 16). 'It seems only GPT-3.5 acquires these heuristics' among the four models tested.

A generic shortcut/spurious-cue risk motivating the whole distractor design: without distractors, given e.g. 'Alex is a cat' and 'All cats are feline' as the only rule of that form, 'the model can deterministically follow the only valid deduction' without actually reasoning over content (Section 3, 'Adding distractors') -- this is presented as a failure mode the dataset is designed to guard against, not an observed model failure per se.

Formal-evaluation footnote 4 describes a labeling pitfall the authors' scorer is built to avoid: a proof can be step-wise 'valid' yet still wrong if a step does not immediately follow its stated premises (worked counterexample: inserting 'All mammals are not herbivorous' as an ungrounded premise) -- included to justify requiring immediate-premise-following, not as an LLM failure but as a scoring-design failure mode the paper explicitly patches (Section 3, footnote 4).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The entire study is single-forward-pass generation (no interactive/multi-turn loop, no per-step model feedback) -- the model receives 8 (or 4, for depth/width experiments) few-shot CoT demonstrations plus the test question in one prompt and produces the full CoT proof in one completion, which is then graded post hoc by the authors' formal step-checker (Section 4, Section 3 'Formal evaluation of chain-of-thought'). Within that single-shot regime, the paper's central 'one-shot vs more demonstration' finding is about the number and DISTRIBUTION of few-shot demonstrations rather than about interaction turns: (1) models can generalize COMPOSITIONALLY from demonstrations of individual (non-compositional) rules to test proofs combining multiple rules, with near-zero ID-OOD accuracy gap in most cases (Section 4.2.2) -- i.e., a form of one-shot-per-call compositional generalization holds without needing compositional demonstrations; (2) but models CANNOT perform proof-by-cases or proof-by-contradiction correctly without at least one in-context demonstration of that specific rule (Section 4.2.1) -- these rule types require explicit demonstration, not just pretraining exposure, to be produced correctly in a single forward pass; (3) demonstration count saturates quickly: 8-shot was used throughout 'since adding further in-context examples did not improve performance' (footnote 5), so more demonstrations beyond ~8 do not help; (4) demonstration distribution matters more than demonstration count or match to test distribution: 'it is not always best to provide demonstrations from the same distribution as the test example' (Section 4.2.2, 4.3) -- OOD (differently-distributed) demonstrations sometimes outperform ID ones, a pattern the authors say is unexplained by existing gradient-descent or Bayesian-inference theories of ICL (Section 5, Conclusion). No claim is made or tested about performance under an interactive/multi-turn correction loop; the paper's entire design is single-shot generation with post hoc automated grading.

relation_to_candidate.position: This paper's protocol is single-shot generation-and-grade: one prompt (few-shot CoT demos + premises + query) in, one full natural-language CoT PROOF SEQUENCE out, graded by an automated FOL-parsing verifier that checks step-wise validity and premise-following (Section 3). This differs from c18 on output shape: PrOntoQA-OOD's primary output is the entire proof chain (a structured sequence of natural-deduction steps), not a single True/False/Unknown label -- the paper explicitly contrasts itself with 'previous datasets that evaluate on a binary true/false answer' and argues PrOntoQA-OOD 'requires LLMs to generate full proofs' instead, because true/false queries introduce an implicit extra negation step that is hard to represent for arbitrary conclusions (Section 3, 'Formal evaluation of chain-of-thought' and footnote 3). c18, by contrast, per its own spec, deliberately drops full proof-chain generation ('Full proof-chain generation as output -- dropped from the design; the adaptation keeps the label only, to bound output tokens and preserve exact-match scoring,' c18.md 'Dead branches') and scores a single T/F/Unknown label by exact match. The verifier type also differs: PrOntoQA-OOD's grader is a formal semantic-parse-and-entailment-check pipeline operating on the generated CoT text (Section 3), whereas c18's ground truth/verifier is the generator's internal proof engine applied to a single label (with the c18 dossier's own skeptic notes flagging that this label is 'definitional,' derived from a probabilistic negation flag rather than an independent theorem-prover verdict, c18.md 'Measurement skeptic').
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Strong family overlap: both use the PrOntoQA lineage's core generative machinery -- fictional/nonce predicates ('wumpus'-style) to block pretraining shortcuts (Section 3, 'Generating proofs with a complete set of deduction rules'; c18.md orientation), controllable proof depth and width as explicit generation parameters (Section 3, 'Varying proof width and depth'; c18's D0-D5 depth bins), distractor sentences/rules to prevent shortcut solutions by forcing a choice among candidate premises (Section 3, 'Adding distractors'; c18's 'distractor rules'), and 8-shot (or fewer) CoT few-shot prompting as the elicitation method (Section 4; c18 does not specify shot count but shares the single-call generate-and-score design). Both are explicitly designed to be contamination-resistant via fresh/fictional symbols per instance. c18.md lists this paper directly as a literature parent: 'PrOntoQA-OOD (Saparov et al., NeurIPS 2023) -- the OOD depth/compositionality extension supplying the incremental-ladder and headroom evidence' (c18.md 'Literature parents').
relation_to_candidate.differences: Three material differences: (1) Output/label shape -- PrOntoQA-OOD scores full proof sequences via a formal step-checker; c18 scores a single T/F/Unknown token via exact match (c18.md explicitly drops full-proof output as a design choice). (2) Deduction-rule breadth -- PrOntoQA-OOD's headline extension over base PrOntoQA is a COMPLETE natural-deduction rule set across all propositional connectives plus COMPOSITIONAL proofs mixing rule types (Section 3, Table 2); c18's 'What it proposes' section (per orientation given) centers on chaining/depth with distractor rules and CWA/negation-as-failure vs explicit negation/Unknown, closer to the base-PrOntoQA / RuleTaker / ProofWriter three-way-label tradition than to PrOntoQA-OOD's rule-completeness and compositional-proof axes. (3) Independent-verifier status -- PrOntoQA-OOD's grader independently re-derives correctness via formal semantic parsing and entailment-checking of the model's own generated proof (an actual verifier over the model's reasoning trace), whereas c18's exact-match label check (absent the proposed forward-chaining fixpoint addition) trusts the generator's stored label, which the c18 dossier itself flags as a soundness gap.
relation_to_candidate.what_c18_would_add: Beyond this paper, c18 would add: (a) a bounded, cheap, single-token scoring target (T/F/Unknown exact match) rather than a full-proof formal-parse pipeline, trading rich step-level diagnosis for much lower per-instance evaluation cost and simpler automation; (b) an explicit open-world Unknown label (CWA-vs-Unknown semantics) which PrOntoQA-OOD does not use -- PrOntoQA-OOD's queries are always provable/disprovable within its natural-deduction framework, not open-world-undetermined; (c) a documented, run-verified, deterministic, byte-identical-at-seed generator with no required repo edits (c18.md 'Repo review & run verification'), an implementation-readiness property not addressed in this paper (which is a research paper, not an infra artifact) though it does share the same underlying asaparov/prontoqa codebase lineage; (d) a proof-engine-named 'failed inference step' for reflection diagnostics at the label level, without requiring the model to output the full proof itself; (e) an added unique-solution constraint-puzzle stratum (CSP/seating-arrangement flavor) entirely outside PrOntoQA-OOD's natural-deduction scope.

corpus_claim: PrOntoQA-OOD (Saparov et al., NeurIPS 2023) is the OOD depth/compositionality extension supplying c18's incremental-ladder and headroom evidence.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The paper genuinely supplies OOD depth-generalization evidence (Section 4.2.3, Figure 7: accuracy decreases with increasing proof depth for both ID and OOD settings when demonstrations are fixed at depth 2) and OOD compositional-generalization evidence (Section 4.2.2, Figure 6: near-zero ID/OOD accuracy gap in most experiments, with documented breakdown at 4 rule types) -- so the 'depth/compositionality extension' and 'headroom evidence' parts of the claim are directly supported by the paper's own stated content and match its self-description ('Our work is closest to PrOntoQA... but extends it to a complete set of deduction rules and to compositional proofs,' Section 2). However, the phrase 'incremental-ladder' is a c18-side framing (referring to c18's own D0-D5 depth bins) that does not map cleanly onto this paper's design: PrOntoQA-OOD's depth/width generalization test (Figure 7) uses a train-depth-2-vs-test-depth-sweep OOD/ID comparison rather than an incremental multi-rung ladder across many depths per se, and this paper's central output is full-proof CoT generation graded by a formal verifier, not a single T/F/Unknown label at each depth rung the way c18's own incremental ladder is framed. The 'headroom evidence' is real but qualitative/figure-based in the extraction (no exact numeric accuracy values were recoverable from the local extraction -- see headline_numbers), so quantitative headroom magnitudes cannot be independently verified from this local file alone. No miscitation of authorship, venue, or core topic was found (title/authors/venue verified exactly, per the source file's own header notes).
baseline_relevant: Depth and width are explicit, independently controllable generation parameters: 'When generating proofs, we control the depth by continuing to append proof steps until a proof of the desired depth is generated. The number of premises of deduction rules is set to the desired proof width' (Section 3, 'Varying proof width and depth').

Specific depth/width values used per rule type in the ID/OOD unseen-rule experiment (Figure 4 caption): implication elimination = width 1, depth 2; conjunction introduction/elimination and disjunction introduction = width 3, depth 2; disjunction elimination = width 3, depth 1; proof by contradiction = width 2, depth 1.

For the distractor experiments (Figure 8 caption), depth/width values differ slightly: conjunction introduction/elimination and disjunction introduction use width 2 (not 3) at depth 2; other rules match Figure 4's values.

Depth/width generalization experiments (Section 4.2.3, Figure 7) fix in-context demonstration depth (or width) at 2 and sweep the test example's depth (or width) upward, using only 4 in-context examples instead of 8 for these specific experiments (Figure 7 caption).

Standard elicitation is 8-shot CoT prompting throughout (not zero-shot, not single-shot without demos), chosen because 'following PrOntoQA, adding further in-context examples did not improve performance' (footnote 5) -- relevant floor/ceiling context: performance saturates by 8 demonstrations.

Metric is 'proof accuracy' (full-proof correctness under the formal step-checker), reported with 95% confidence intervals for all results, over 100 trials per experiment/condition: 'We run each experiment for 100 trials and measure the accuracy of the output proofs' (Section 4.1); '95% confidence intervals are provided for all results' (Section 4).

Contamination resistance via fictional/nonce vocabulary is explicit design intent, directly supporting c18's contamination-resistance claim: 'To prevent LLMs from exploiting knowledge from pretraining to solve the problems without reasoning, we use fictional names for all concepts (e.g. "wumpus" instead of "cat" etc.)' (Section 3).

Data/code release status (relevant for baseline reuse/regeneration): 'All analysis code, data, data generation scripts, and model outputs are publicly available at github.com/asaparov/prontoqa' (Section 1, footnote 1); reproducibility statement confirms 'model outputs (except those of PaLM), code for data generation, and analysis code are freely available' and 'The generated data for all experiments in this paper is available in the file generated_ood_data.zip' (Reproducibility statement) -- this is the same repository c18's implementation plan is built on and run-verified against.

GPT-3.5 experiments used OpenAI API model text-davinci-003, run April 20-23, 2023; Figure 7 and part of Figure 8 experiments run August 3-7, 2023 (Reproducibility statement) -- useful for judging whether any published PrOntoQA-OOD outputs could now be contamination risks for current frontier models, though c18's own design regenerates fresh instances rather than reusing released data (c18.md 'Dead branches': 'Reuse of published RuleTaker/ProofWriter/PrOntoQA sets verbatim -- refused because the released theories are contaminated').

A concrete floor/failure example useful for baseline sanity-checking distractor design: without distractors, a single valid deduction path is 'deterministic' and thus trivially exploitable without real reasoning (Section 3, 'Adding distractors') -- confirms that a no-distractor baseline instance risks measuring shortcut-following rather than reasoning, which is directly relevant to how c18 should size its distractor rules.
quotes: "Given the intractably large size of the space of proofs, any model that is capable of general deductive reasoning must generalize to proofs of greater complexity." (Abstract)

"To prevent LLMs from exploiting knowledge from pretraining to solve the problems without reasoning, we use fictional names for all concepts (e.g. “wumpus” instead of “cat” etc.)." (Section 3, 'Generating proofs with a complete set of deduction rules')
"Unlike previous datasets that evaluate on a binary true/false answer, PrOntoQA-OOD requires LLMs to generate full proofs." (Section 3, 'Formal evaluation of chain-of-thought')
"In all experiments, we use 8-shot chain-of-thought prompting... Following PrOntoQA, since adding further in-context examples did not improve performance." (Section 4 and footnote 5)
"However, no model is able to use disjunction elimination and proof by contradiction without demonstrations." (Section 4.2.1)

"In Figure 6, in all but three experiments, we observe that the gap in proof accuracy between the ID and OOD settings is close to zero, indicating that the models are able to generalize compositionally to an extent." (Section 4.2.2)

"As is evident from Figure 7, when shown demonstrations of proofs of depth 2, the models' performance decreases with increasing depth. But this is due to the increase in the inherent difficulty of the task, as both ID and OOD accuracies decrease with increasing depth." (Section 4.2.3)

"The models' performance is largely unaffected, with the exception of a few rules for specific models. This is in stark contrast to supervised learning, where it is always best to train on examples from the same distribution as the test example." (Section 4.3)
